name: Cadmium
name_origin: From the Latin 'cadmia' and Greek 'kadmeia', the ancient name for calamine (zinc carbonate ore), reflecting the element's close association with zinc; ultimately derived from the legendary Phoenician prince Cadmus.

discovery.discoverer: Friedrich Stromeyer; independently by Karl Samuel Leberecht Hermann

occurrence.abundance_summary: Cadmium is rare in Earth's crust at about 0.15 ppm. It is almost never found in pure form; instead it occurs as a minor component (typically 0.01-0.05%) in zinc ores such as sphalerite (ZnS), and is recovered as a byproduct of zinc, lead, and copper smelting.
states: bound in sphalerite (ZnS) as a minor substituent
byproduct of zinc and lead refining

compounds.name: Cadmium sulfide
compounds.summary: A yellow-orange solid (the mineral greenockite) used historically as the pigment 'cadmium yellow' and in photoresistors and thin-film solar cells.

compounds.name: Cadmium selenide
compounds.summary: A semiconductor used to make quantum dots for displays, biomedical imaging, and photovoltaics.

compounds.name: Cadmium telluride
compounds.summary: A direct-bandgap semiconductor used in commercial thin-film photovoltaic modules.

compounds.name: Cadmium oxide
compounds.summary: A brown solid used as a transparent conducting oxide and in some specialty electronics.

compounds.name: Cadmium hydroxide
compounds.summary: A white solid that serves as the negative electrode material in nickel-cadmium rechargeable batteries.

uses: Nickel-cadmium (NiCd) rechargeable batteries (a use being phased out due to toxicity)
Cadmium telluride thin-film solar panels for utility-scale photovoltaics
Quantum dots (CdSe) for displays and biological labeling
Anti-corrosion electroplating on steel fasteners and aerospace components
Pigments (cadmium yellow, orange, and red) in artist paints, plastics, and ceramics
Control rods and shielding in nuclear reactors due to high neutron-capture cross section
reactivity_summary: Cadmium is a soft, bluish-white, malleable transition metal that tarnishes slowly in moist air to form a protective oxide. It dissolves readily in dilute mineral acids, especially nitric acid, but is resistant to alkalis. Cadmium burns in air at high temperature with a brown flame to give cadmium oxide. It and its compounds are highly toxic; cadmium is a recognized human carcinogen and a major environmental pollutant.
isotopes_summary: Cadmium has eight naturally occurring isotopes: 106Cd (~1.25%), 108Cd (~0.89%), 110Cd (~12.49%), 111Cd (~12.80%), 112Cd (~24.13%), 113Cd (~12.22%, very long-lived), 114Cd (~28.73%), and 116Cd (~7.49%, double beta decay). 113Cd has a half-life of about 8x10^15 years. The synthetic isotope 109Cd (462 days) is used in X-ray fluorescence calibration.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Cadmium
